This stage sits in the app server’s integration-test layer, exercising the extensibility and tool-execution surfaces that the main request/response loop depends on once the server is running. It verifies that external content can be discovered, installed, scoped, invoked, and cleaned up correctly across local filesystems, remote backends, and per-thread runtime state.

The plugin and marketplace tests cover the full lifecycle: listing and reading plugin metadata and skills, adding/removing/upgrading marketplaces, installing and uninstalling plugins, and sharing plugins through backend APIs. Together they validate catalog synchronization, bundle layout, auth and OAuth startup, analytics, cache/config cleanup, and error handling. App, hooks, and skills suites then confirm that installed extensions become visible through app discovery, hook enumeration, filesystem watching, and runtime config changes.

Executor-focused tests prove that plugin-provided skills and MCP servers can be exposed only to threads that select a capability root. The MCP suites cover server status, direct tool calls, elicitation forwarding, and resource reads. Command and shell-command tests validate process execution modes, approvals, environment handling, and history rules. Finally, extension-backed tools such as image generation, sleep, web search, and fuzzy file search confirm that model-issued tool calls produce the expected notifications, outputs, and persisted state.
